name: Tallow Worker's Reinforced Coat
description: A heavy waxed canvas coat worn by Tallow Ring workers handling hot product — the multiple layered panels at the torso offer modest protection without advertising armor to anyone looking.

name: Waste-Runner's Padded Vest
description: A salvaged garment rebuilt from layered cloth, raw leather, and whatever rigid scrap could be stitched inside the panels — standard kit for anyone who goes beyond the gates and expects to come back.

name: Apothecary's Treated Duster
description: A long leather coat impregnated with alum, coal-tar oil, and one other compound its maker has not disclosed — worn by compound workers and black-market chemists who expect proximity to reactive substances.

name: Compact Watchman's Chainmail Vest
description: Issued to active Compact watchmen on district assignment — a riveted mail vest worn under a tabard with the ward colors, returned to the armory at end of service rotation or recovered from the body.

name: League Foreman's Half-Plate
description: A composite of brigandine chest-panels and spaulders issued to League floor-supervisors who need to walk the smelting halls during labor disputes — not elegant, but made by people who know what metal can do.

name: Harbor Ward Boarding Coat
description: Oiled canvas over a hidden layer of ring-mail, shaped for movement on wet dock planking — Harbor ward enforcers use these when they board vessels for compliance inspections that sometimes end badly.

name: Salvaged Pre-Breach Plate (Partial)
description: Chest, gorget, and pauldrons recovered from a sealed lower-district armory — the steel predates the Breach and is visibly superior to current production, though the matching leg-harness was not found.

name: League Armory Chest-Shell
description: A full breastplate produced by the League's private armorers for senior enforcement officers — the design borrows from pre-Breach technical drawings and is not commercially available, only issued or confiscated.

name: Ring Courier's Silent-Tread Leather
description: A precisely fitted leather harness made by a Ring-affiliated cobbler — the sole inlays muffle footfall on stone, the cut allows full arm rotation, and the fit is taken from measurements, not stock sizes.

name: Compact Assessor's Reinforced Coat
description: A formal-cut wool coat worn by Compact property assessors in contested districts — the inner panels are padded with layers of densely woven scrap-metal rings, invisible unless the wearer is searched.

name: Basic Padded Vest
description: Layers of rough cloth stitched over a wool core — worn by anyone who expects trouble and cannot afford better. It will not stop a blade. It will slow one.

name: Studded Leather Haubergeon
description: Short jacket of hardened leather with iron studs across the chest and shoulders — standard equipment for ward-hired guards and anyone who does rough work without institutional backing.

name: Scrivener's Padded Overcoat
description: A clerk's long coat with a double-layered chest and back — unremarkable to look at, which is precisely why certain people wear it. Provides minimal protection but raises no questions at a gate check.

name: Tanner's Stiff Hide
description: A chest piece cut from thick tannery-grade hide, boiled and shaped without much care for comfort — it keeps its shape, resists cutting, and smells faintly of the process that made it.

name: Patchwork Cuirass
description: Assembled from mismatched leather panels, scrap iron strips, and whatever cord held it together — built in the Warrens by someone who needed something and had nothing. It shows. It still works.

name: Riveted Chain Shirt
description: A waist-length shirt of riveted iron rings, sold in most hardware markets without restriction. Not fashionable, not conspicuous, and harder to damage than anything a common blade will attempt.

name: Ring-and-Plate Combination
description: Chest plates over a mail underlayer, assembled by someone who knew what they were doing and had access to mismatched components — favored by independent contractors who cannot requisition from an armory.

name: Laminar Riding Vest
description: Overlapping horizontal strips of hardened leather riveted to a canvas backing — designed for movement rather than standing in a line. Couriers and outriders use these because they bend where plate does not.

name: Segmented Chest Harness
description: A series of articulated iron panels running front and back, joined by leather hinges — allows better vertical movement than solid plate while providing reasonable coverage. A working design, not an elegant one.

name: Iron-Banded Armor
description: Horizontal iron bands over leather backing, running from collar to hip — the upper edge of medium protection. Worn by senior enforcement staff and anyone who expects to walk into a bad situation and walk back out.

name: Full Issue Plate
description: A complete set of fitted iron plate covering chest, back, arms, and thighs — the kind issued to the personal guard of Houses with the resources to equip them properly. Heavy, obvious, and intended to be both.

name: Tower-Backed Mail Suit
description: Full mail coat worn under a front plate and pauldrons — a design from the wall-defense doctrine of the pre-Breach period, surplus from armories that no longer have garrisons to fill them.

name: Forged Iron Harness
description: Chest and back plates forged in the Ironworks district to a practical specification rather than an aesthetic one — the seams are ugly, the fit is approximate, and it will stop most of what gets swung at it.

name: Sigil Plate (House Issue)
description: A full plate harness bearing the embossed mark of a Court house — the protection is excellent and the workmanship is genuine, but wearing it without affiliation is a political statement someone will eventually question.

name: Lord's Panoply (Repossessed)
description: A complete fitted panoply — gorget, cuirass, pauldrons, gauntlets, and tassets — seized from a collapsed house and sold at debt auction. The craftsmanship predates the current generation. There is no better protection available on the open market.

name: Scrap-Iron Shell
description: Scavenged iron panels wired and bolted together over thick leather — assembled for protection, not comfort. Heavy for what it stops, fragile for what it cost, and impossible to mistake for legitimate gear.

name: Bone-Laced Vest
description: A leather chest piece reinforced with interlocked bone splints — the origin of the material is not discussed in reputable markets. Lighter than metal alternatives and surprisingly effective against bladed weapons.

name: Treated Rags
description: Cloth strips layered and soaked in a hardening compound — thin protection at best, no protection at worst. Used by people who need something between themselves and a blade and have no other options.

name: Merchant's Proof Coat
description: A merchant's traveling coat with hidden panels of dense weave and thin iron plate at the chest — looks like a normal travel garment, passes a casual inspection, and stops the kind of blade that gets used in business disputes.

name: Officer's Regimentals
description: A formal uniform coat with structured shoulders and reinforced paneling — the cut reads as official even without insignia. Compact officers wear these on district assignments where appearing in armor would invite the wrong questions.
